behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: Failure risk is at the boundary between catalog availability data and ranking/presentation logic. A unit test of filter code can pass while wiring bugs (wrong join, stale availability field, or post-rank filtering omission) still leak out-of-stock items.
behaviors.test_description: Run the recommendation pipeline end-to-end in-process with a real local datastore fixture containing mixed stock states and high relevance scores for out-of-stock items; assert no returned item is out-of-stock.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: Real failures commonly come from preference retrieval/mapping plus filter composition, not just predicate logic. Pure mocks can hide schema/serialization mismatches in user settings.
behaviors.test_description: Use real preference records in a local DB and run recommendation generation across modules; assert blocked categories are absent even when products are otherwise highly ranked.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: This is a ranking-quality claim. If ranking depends on a real ML recommender/scorer, mocked scorers can always satisfy ordering and miss real model behavior drift/variance.
behaviors.test_description: With a fixed user/profile fixture, call the real recommendation model API and compare rank positions of browsing-matched vs no-signal products across repeated runs; require matched products to be consistently promoted above controls within tolerance.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: The main failure boundary is query/sort/pagination wiring. Unit tests can miss unstable ordering from datastore ties or cursor/offset implementation details.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a local datastore with deterministic fixtures including tie-score cases, request page 2 twice via the full recommendation service path, and assert identical ordered IDs and correct window boundaries.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: This is a recommendation-quality objective on ranking composition. Mocked rankers can trivially force serendipity and hide whether the real model/exploration strategy actually produces it.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the real recommender for users with narrow historical categories and assert top-N includes at least one item from an unseen category over repeated trials, tracking pass rate against a defined threshold.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: Cold-start fallback usually depends on combining user-state detection with popularity data source access. Unit logic alone can miss missing-join/null-state wiring failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create a true new-user record with no events in local DB, run full recommendation generation, and assert non-empty results align with current popularity ordering source.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: Risk sits in event payload construction plus persistence schema. Mocked sinks can hide field-mapping and timestamp serialization defects.
behaviors.test_description: Serve a recommendation list, simulate click events through the real handler, and verify persisted tracking rows contain exact product ID, displayed rank position, and correctly formatted timestamp.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: Latency SLO depends on runtime, process, OS scheduling, memory, and datastore behavior. In-process mocks or microbench units can hide true bottlenecks.
behaviors.test_description: Run a production-like service process and datastore with 100k-product fixture, execute realistic concurrent recommendation requests, and assert p95 (or specified percentile) latency remains under 200ms.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: This requires sequential stateful behavior across multiple recommendation generations with an intervening purchase update. If the recommender is model-driven, correctness depends on multi-step agentic pipeline behavior, not a single call.
behaviors.test_description: Execute full sequence: generate recommendations via real model, record a purchase of a surfaced item, trigger state update path, then generate recommendations again and assert purchased item rank is materially lower (or excluded) per policy.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: Determinism here is a cross-call property. When real model scoring is involved, you need repeated end-to-end calls under unchanged state to expose stochastic drift that mocks would hide.
behaviors.test_description: Freeze user/catalog state, issue two back-to-back recommendation requests through the full stack (real scorer enabled), and assert identical ranked product IDs and scores; repeat across multiple trials to detect intermittent nondeterminism.
